summary: Jeff Atwood examines Joel Spolsky's argument about whether hiring the best programmers matters, citing data from a Yale professor showing no correlation between time spent on programming assignments and grades achieved. While acknowledging the well-documented skill disparity in software development, Atwood pushes back on Joel's conclusion by noting it applies mainly to shrink-wrap software companies—a tiny slice of the overall IT market. For the vast majority of in-house and internal software development, hiring 'rock stars' may not be justified or necessary.
key_points: Extreme skill disparity is unique to software development, with roughly equal odds of working with a genius or a jackass
There is no correlation between experience and programming skill—talented interns can outperform 20-year veterans
Yale CS data shows no correlation between time spent on assignments and resulting grades
Joel's argument about needing the best programmers applies mainly to commercial shrink-wrap software, not the broader IT market
For internal/in-house software development, hiring 'rock stars' is rarely justified
money_quotes: Extreme skill disparity is unique to the profession of software development.
The odds of working with a genius or a jackass on any given job are about fifty-fifty.
There's no correlation between experience and skill, either. I've worked with amazingly talented interns and 20 year vets who produced terrible code.
There is absolutely no correlation whatsoever between time spent on the problem and the resulting score for these CS students.
Nobody hires Dolly Parton to sing at weddings.
Since Joel's commercial shrink-wrap software development is a tiny percentage of the overall IT market, does this mean that having the best programmers doesn't matter most of the time?
Sadly, I believe the answer is yes.
key_insight: While top programming talent creates outsized value in product companies, the uncomfortable truth is that most software work doesn't require—or justify—hiring the best.
